column4: What is the primary function of a wave as it propagates?
column6: Transfers energy from one location to another
column7: Creates new particles in a medium
column10: A wave is defined as a disturbance in matter that transfers energy from one location to another.

column4: Which type of wave requires a medium to travel through?
column10: Waves that need a medium are known as mechanical waves while those that can travel without a medium are called electromagnetic waves.

column4: If you are on the Moon which has no atmosphere why could you see a light but not hear a sound?
column5: Light is mechanical and sound is electromagnetic
column6: Light is electromagnetic and sound is mechanical
column10: Light can be seen because electromagnetic waves do not need a medium whereas sound (mechanical) requires an atmosphere to travel.

column4: In a transverse wave how do the particles of the medium move relative to the wave propagation?
column8: They do not move at all
column10: In a transverse wave the particles of the medium move perpendicularly to the direction the wave is traveling.

column4: What are the highest points in a transverse wave called?
column10: As illustrated in Figure 1 the highest points in a transverse wave are known as crests.

column4: Which region in a longitudinal wave corresponds to particles being farthest apart?
column10: As depicted in Figure 2b expansion (or rarefaction) is where the particles of the medium are farthest apart.

column4: What is the SI unit for frequency?
column10: The SI unit for frequency (f) is s^-1 or Hertz (Hz).

column4: Which wave property is defined as the time it takes for one complete cycle to pass?
column10: Period (T) is the time elapsed between two consecutive crests passing by the same point; the time it takes for one complete cycle.

column4: If a wave has a frequency of 10 Hz what is its period?
column10: The relationship is T = 1/f. Therefore 1 divided by 10 Hz equals 0.1 seconds.

column4: How is wave speed (v) calculated using wavelength and frequency?
column10: The wave speed can be expressed as v = λf as seen in Equation 2.

column4: A wave has a wavelength of 2.50 m and a frequency of 3.00 Hz. What is its speed?
column10: Using the formula v = λf (2.50 m * 3.00 Hz) the wave speed is 7.50 m/s.

column4: What happens to the speed of a wave on a string if the tension is increased?
column10: Based on Equation 3 increasing the tension (Ft) in the string increases the wave speed.

column4: The speed of a transverse wave on a string depends on which two factors?
column7: Tension and mass per unit length
column8: Color and length of the string
column10: The speed of a transverse wave on a stretched string depends on the tension (Ft) and on the mass per unit length (μ).

column4: What is the symbol used for mass per unit length (linear mass density)?
column10: μ (mu) is the symbol representing mass per unit length where μ = m/l.

column4: A spring toy vibrates at 2 Hz. What is the period of the wave produced?
column10: Using T = 1/f the period is 1 / 2 Hz which equals 0.5 s.

column4: If the distance between two crests is 7.0 m what is this property called?
column10: Wavelength (λ) is defined as the distance between two consecutive crests.

column4: Light travels at 3.00 x 10^8 m/s. If yellow light has a wavelength of 5.45 x 10^-7 m what is its frequency?
column10: Frequency is speed divided by wavelength (3.00 x 10^8 / 5.45 x 10^-7) which is approximately 5.50 x 10^14 Hz.

column4: What type of wave is light?
column10: Light is an electromagnetic wave and can travel without a medium.

column4: A 300 m wire has a mass of 15 kg and a tension of 1000 N. What is its linear mass density (μ)?
column10: Linear mass density μ is mass divided by length (15 kg / 300 m) which equals 0.050 kg/m.

column4: How would wave speed change if a string were made heavier (thicker/denser) while tension remained the same?
column10: Since wave speed is v = sqrt(Ft / μ) increasing the mass per unit length (μ) in the denominator causes the speed to decrease.

column4: What is a line drawn perpendicular to wave fronts to indicate the direction of motion?
column10: A ray is a line drawn perpendicular to the wave fronts to indicate the direction at which the wave fronts are moving towards.

column4: What happens to spherical wave fronts as they travel very far from their point source?
column10: As waves travel farther from their source they eventually lose almost all their curvature until all wave fronts become planar.

column4: According to the Law of Reflection how does the angle of incidence (i) relate to the angle of reflection (r)?
column10: The law of reflection states that the angles of incidence and reflection are equal to each other at every point.

column4: What is the term for reflection off a rough uneven surface?
column10: If a light wave hits a rough uneven surface
column11: then the reflection is considered to be diffuse.

column4: What occurs when a wave crosses an interface and its speed changes?
column10: Depending on the nature and conditions of the medium
column11: the speed of a wave can vary. This wave phenomena is called refraction.

column4: What happens to the refracted angle when a wave moves into a medium where it travels slower?
column10: If the wave crosses an interface towards a medium where the wave’s speed becomes slower
column11: the refracted angle will be smaller.

column4: What is the result when two wave pulses with the same orientation overlap to form a higher peak?
column10: When waves interact to form higher peaks as their amplitudes add up together

column4: In destructive interference what happens to the amplitude when two opposite pulses overlap?
column10: Destructive interference completely collapses the amplitude to zero during the interference of the two waves.

column4: Regarding interference what happens to fringes when two point sources are moved closer together?
column10: When the sources are closer together

column4: What is the process of wave fronts forming new circular waves after passing through a gap?
column10: When wave fronts pass through a gap or slit on a barrier... this process of formation of wave fronts in this manner is called diffraction.

column4: Under what condition do diffracted wave fronts appear more spherical?
column5: The slit is wider than the wavelength
column6: The slit is narrower than the wavelength
column10: If the slit’s width is narrower when compared to the incident wave front’s wavelength
column11: then the diffracted wave fronts are more spherical.

column4: What device is used in the lab to demonstrate reflection and refraction using water?
column10: The study guide outlines experiments for demonstrating wave phenomena using a ripple tank setup.

column4: Which tool is used in the simulation to vary the frequency of wave generation?
column10: Using the settings on the simulation you can vary the frequency of the wave generator.

column4: What is the "normal line" in wave reflection?
column5: A line parallel to the surface
column6: A line perpendicular to the surface
column10: The normal line is a perpendicular line with respect to the surface used to measure angles of incidence and reflection.

column4: If a wave crosses an interface parallel to the normal line what happens to its direction?
column7: It continues parallel to the normal line
column10: If the wave crosses an interface parallel to the normal line towards any arbitrary medium
column11: the refracted wave will also travel parallel to the normal line.

column4: In the fiber optic example what phenomenon allows light to stay trapped inside the cable?
column10: Signals in terms of light waves bounce around the cables by being subjected to repeated reflections that do not diminish amplitude.

column4: What is a wave front?
column6: A series of points forming a crest
column10: A wave front is a series of connected points on a wave that form the wave crest.

column4: How does a wider slit affect a planar wave front during diffraction?
column7: It keeps the wave front practically planar
column10: A wider slit does not pinch the planar wave front hard enough
column11: which is why the diffracted waves look unaltered and practically keep its planar shape.

column4: What happens to a wave after it has finished interfering with another wave?
column7: It continues in its direction with properties intact
column8: It turns into a standing wave
column10: Even though the waves have interfered with each other
column11: they continue to propagate to their intended direction keeping all of their properties intact.

column4: What application is used on a smartphone to ensure the ripple tank is level?
column10: Install the phyphox application... use the inclination meter to make the platform level.

column4: What causes sound to be produced?
column10: Sound is produced by vibrations or mechanical movements which cause nearby particles in a medium to vibrate.

column4: Why is it impossible for sound to travel through a vacuum?
column6: There are no particles to carry the vibrations
column8: A vacuum has too much pressure
column10: A medium is essential for sound transmission as its molecules must be present to carry these vibrations.

column4: Which process occurs when a speaker diaphragm moves to the right and pushes particles together?
column10: When the diaphragm moves to the right it pushes the medium’s particles producing the dark patterns or compressions.

column4: What type of wave is a sound wave?
column10: Sound waves are longitudinal meaning the back-and-forth movement of the medium is in the same direction as the traveling wave.

column4: In a longitudinal wave what are the regions where particles are spread out called?
column10: When the speaker diaphragm moves to the left it pulls the medium’s particles producing light patterns or rarefactions.

column4: How is the pitch of a sound related to its frequency?
column10: Higher frequencies mean more waves per second and result in a higher pitch.

column4: How does increased air density affect the air pressure and loudness?
column10: Higher air density means more molecules bumping into each other creating higher pressure and making the sound louder.

column4: In which of the following media does sound travel the fastest?
column10: The closer the molecules are to each other the easier for vibrations to travel. This is why sound travels fastest in solids.

column4: Why does sound travel faster in warm air than in cold air?
column6: Particles move faster and bump into each other more often
column10: Increased temperature causes particles to move faster and bump into one another more often which is why sound travels faster in warm air.

column4: What is a distinct reflection of sound heard after a specific delay?
column10: An echo is a distinct reflection of sound heard after a delay.

column4: What is the difference between an echo and a reverberation?
column6: Reverberations have no recognizable time delay
column8: Reverberations only happen in a vacuum
column10: Reverberation is a blend of many reflections that overlap with no recognizable time delay between the original and reflected sound.

column4: Which surface type would reflect the most sound energy?
column10: More energy is reflected when the surface is rigid than when the surface is soft and rough.

column4: What is the process called when animals use reflected sound to navigate and find prey?
column10: Echolocation is a process used by some animals like bats and dolphins to locate objects using sound.

column4: What does the acronym SONAR stand for?
column10: Sonar stands for Sound wave Navigation And Ranging.

column4: What does a sonar system measure to determine the distance to an object?
column6: The time it takes for the echo to return
column8: The frequency shift of the water
column10: The animal’s brain (or sonar system) processes the time it took for the echo to return to determine location and distance.

column4: Which medical technology uses the principle of echolocation to see inside the body?
column10: Echolocation principles are used in ultrasound imaging which allows doctors to probe inside a patient’s body.

column4: Why is high-frequency sound better for detecting small objects?
column6: It provides more detail for spotting small nearby objects
column10: High-frequency sounds are better for spotting small nearby objects with more detail.

column4: According to the guide why can you hear a train through the metal rails before the air?
column6: The rails are longer than the air
column7: Sound travels faster in solids than in gases
column10: Sound travels faster in solids (metal rails) than in gases (air).

column4: If the frequency of a sound wave is 100 Hz what does this mean?
column5: The wave travels 100 meters per second
column6: 100 waves pass a fixed point each second
column7: The sound is 100 times louder than normal
column10: The frequency of a sound refers to how many waves pass a fixed point each second.

column4: Which of the following is an example of an echo-locating animal?
column10: Echolocation is a special capability used by bats

column4: Who was the early thinker that proposed light consists of "visual rays" coming from our eyes?
column10: Around 300 BCE Euclid suggested straight-line visual rays emanated from the eye to perceive objects.

column4: How did Ibn al-Haytham change the understanding of vision in 1021?
column5: He proved light is a wave
column6: He argued that light enters the eye rather than leaving it
column8: He measured the speed of light
column10: Ibn al-Haytham provided a key shift in cause and effect by arguing light enters the eye to produce vision.

column4: What was Isaac Newton's primary argument regarding the nature of light in 1666?
column6: Light is composed of tiny particles called corpuscles
column10: Newton used a prism to study light and championed the "Corpuscular Theory" where light is made of particles.

column4: Which phenomenon observed by Thomas Young in 1801 provided the strongest evidence for the wave model?
column10: Young's double-slit experiment showed alternating bright and dark fringes which are characteristic of wave interference.

column4: According to the particle model what would you expect to see on a screen behind a double-slit?
column10: If light were only particles they would travel in straight lines through the slits and create two patches of light rather than interference patterns.

column4: What did James Clerk Maxwell predict about light in 1864?
column5: It is a stream of photons
column7: It requires a physical medium to travel
column10: Maxwell showed that light consists of changing electric and magnetic fields making it an electromagnetic wave.

column4: Which two types of radiation are the immediate neighbors of visible light on the EM spectrum?
column10: Visible light sits between infrared (lower frequency) and ultraviolet (higher frequency) on the electromagnetic spectrum.

column4: In the photoelectric effect what happens if the light frequency is below the threshold frequency?
column6: Only very bright light ejects electrons
column7: No electrons are ejected regardless of brightness
column10: Below the threshold frequency individual photons do not have enough energy to eject an electron no matter how many photons (brightness) there are.

column4: What is a "photon" as defined by Einstein's work?
column10: Einstein proposed that light energy comes in discrete packets called photons to explain the photoelectric effect.

column4: As you move from Red to Violet light across the spectrum what happens to the properties?
column10: Moving from red to violet results in a shorter wavelength and a higher frequency which carries more energy.

column4: Why does a high-frequency dim light eject electrons while a low-frequency bright light does not?
column5: Dim light has more total energy
column6: Individual photons in high-frequency light have enough energy to overcome the threshold
column7: Bright light reflects off the metal too quickly
column10: The photoelectric effect depends on the energy of individual photons (determined by frequency) rather than the total number of photons (brightness).

column4: What is the name of the theory that describes light as having both wave and particle properties?
column10: The modern understanding that light behaves as both a wave (interference) and a particle (photons) is called wave-particle duality.

column4: Which scientist is credited with the wavefront idea in 1690?
column10: Christiaan Huygens proposed the wavefront model to explain how light propagates as a wave.

column4: What provides the "bright bands" in Young's double-slit experiment?
column10: Bright bands occur when wave crests overlap and add together through constructive interference.

column4: Which technology is most directly linked to the discovery of the photoelectric effect and photons?
column10: Solar cells (and digital camera sensors) work by converting light photons into electrical current via the photoelectric effect.

column4: In the PHET simulation what is the effect of increasing light intensity at a frequency above the threshold?
column5: It increases the speed of electrons
column6: It increases the number of electrons ejected
column8: It changes the color of the metal
column10: Increased intensity means more photons which results in more electrons being knocked out as long as the frequency is sufficient.

column4: Who developed the first ruby laser in 1960?
column10: Theodore Maiman developed the first working laser which is a technology that relies on the photon model of light.

column4: What does "threshold frequency" refer to in the context of light?
column5: The speed of light in a vacuum
column6: The maximum frequency of visible light
column7: The minimum frequency needed to eject electrons from a metal
column8: The frequency of a radio wave
column10: Threshold frequency is the minimum energy level required for a photon to dislodge an electron from a specific metal.

column4: If you are explaining why light spreads when passing through a small opening which model should you use?
column10: The wave model explains diffraction which is the spreading or bending of waves as they pass through an opening.

column4: Which of the following best describes the historical "turning point" involving Maxwell and Hertz?
column6: Confirming light is an electromagnetic wave
column8: Proving light is a longitudinal wave like sound
column10: Maxwell predicted light was an EM wave and Hertz's later experiments with radio waves confirmed this model.

column4: What are the three primary colors of light that our eyes are most sensitive to?
column10: Our eyes have three types of cone cells that are specifically sensitive to red

column4: According to the additive color model what color is produced when red and green light are mixed at full intensity?
column10: Mixing red and green light activates both the red and green cones in our eyes which the brain perceives as yellow.

column4: If all three primary colors of light (Red Green and Blue) are combined at 100% intensity what is the resulting color?
column10: White light is the combination of all visible frequencies; when RGB bulbs are all at full power they simulate white light.

column4: What happens to the colors of light that do NOT match the natural vibration frequency of an object’s atoms?
column10: If the light frequency does not match the atoms' vibration it is not absorbed and instead reflects off the surface to our eyes.

column4: What is the name of the special cells in the retina that allow us to see color?
column10: Cones are the photoreceptor cells responsible for color vision whereas rods are more sensitive to low light levels.

column4: Why does a yellow banana appear black when viewed under pure blue light?
column5: Blue light is reflected by the banana
column6: The banana absorbs the blue light and reflects nothing
column7: Blue and yellow make black light
column10: A yellow banana reflects red and green light but absorbs blue. With only blue light hitting it there is no red or green to reflect so it looks dark.

column4: Which phenomenon is primarily responsible for the sky appearing blue during the day?
column10: Blue light travels in shorter smaller waves and is scattered in all directions by gas molecules in the atmosphere more than other colors.

column4: Why does the sun look red or orange during a sunset?
column6: The light travels through more atmosphere scattering away the blue and green light
column7: The sun moves closer to the Earth
column8: Red light is absorbed by the clouds
column10: At sunset sunlight travels a longer path through the atmosphere which scatters away the shorter blue wavelengths leaving the longer red and orange wavelengths.

column4: What color is produced by mixing Red and Blue light?
column10: In the additive color mixing process red light plus blue light creates the color magenta.

column4: What color is produced by mixing Blue and Green light?
column10: Combining blue and green light creates cyan which is the primary color of the subtractive system but a secondary color in light mixing.

column4: Why do thunderclouds appear dark compared to regular white clouds?
column5: They are made of black water
column6: They are much thicker and block more sunlight from passing through
column8: They absorb all the heat from the sun
column10: Because thunderclouds are very thick and dense with water droplets they block and absorb most sunlight rather than scattering it through.

column4: What happens when you mix yellow paint and blue paint?
column10: Paint uses subtractive color mixing where yellow and blue pigments together reflect the green part of the spectrum.

column4: Which acronym is commonly used to remember the sequence of colors in white light?
column10: The sequence of colors from longest to shortest wavelength is Red Orange Yellow Green Blue Indigo and Violet.

column4: If an object absorbs all colors of the visible spectrum what color does it appear to be?
column10: An object appears black when it absorbs all frequencies of visible light and reflects none back to the eye.

column4: How does smog or air pollution affect the appearance of the sky?
column5: It makes the sky brighter blue
column6: It absorbs and scatters light making the sky look hazy and dark
column8: It has no effect on light
column10: Particulates in smog absorb and scatter light differently than clean air molecules often resulting in a brownish or grey hazy appearance.

column4: Which device uses the additive color mixing of Red Green and Blue to create images?
column10: Screens use tiny RGB pixels to mix light and create the full range of colors we see digitally.

column4: What did Isaac Newton use to demonstrate that white light is a mixture of colors?
column10: Newton used a prism to refract white light into its component colors showing that white light is composite.

column4: Which color of visible light has the highest frequency and shortest wavelength?
column10: Violet light sits at the end of the visible spectrum with the highest frequency and the most energy per photon.

column4: What color would a red apple appear to be under pure green light?
column10: A red apple reflects red light and absorbs green. Since there is no red light to reflect it will appear black.

column4: What is the process called when light passes through a material like a clear glass window?
column10: Transmission occurs when light waves pass through a medium rather than being absorbed or bouncing off.

column4: What was the significance of Young’s double-slit experiment?
column5: It proved light is a particle
column6: It proved light behaves as a wave
column7: It measured the speed of light
column10: Young's experiment showed interference patterns which are a unique characteristic of wave behavior.

column4: According to the law of reflection how does the angle of incidence compare to the angle of reflection?
column5: The angle of incidence is always larger
column6: The angle of incidence is always smaller
column7: The angles are equal to each other
column8: The angles always add up to 180 degrees
column10: The law of reflection states that the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection as measured from the normal line.

column4: What is the invisible line called that stands straight up (90 degrees) from a reflecting surface?
column10: The normal line is a perpendicular reference line used to measure angles of incidence reflection and refraction.

column4: Why does light bend (refract) when it moves from air into a piece of glass?
column6: The light slows down as it enters the denser material
column8: The light is being reflected by atoms
column10: Refraction occurs because light changes speed when passing from one medium into another of a different density.

column4: What causes the "dispersion" of white light into a rainbow when passing through a prism?
column5: The prism adds color to the light
column6: Each color of light bends at a slightly different angle due to different speeds
column7: The prism reflects the light in one direction
column8: The light is absorbed by the glass
column10: Dispersion happens because different wavelengths (colors) of light travel at different speeds in glass causing them to refract at different angles.

column4: In an interference pattern what do the bright lines represent?
column10: Bright lines or fringes occur where the crests of light waves overlap and add together which is constructive interference.

column4: How does the diffraction pattern of light through a single slit differ from a double slit?
column5: Single slit has no dark bands
column6: Single slit has a much wider central bright spot compared to its side spots
column7: Double slit has no center bright spot
column8: There is no difference between them
column10: Single-slit diffraction is characterized by a broad central maximum while double-slit interference produces more evenly spaced fringes.

column4: Why can you see your reflection in a mirror but not in a piece of white paper?
column6: The mirror is silver and the paper is white
column7: The mirror has a smooth surface that reflects light in an organized way
column8: The paper is too thin to reflect light
column10: Smooth surfaces produce specular reflection (organized) while rough surfaces like paper cause diffuse reflection (scattered).

column4: What is the "critical angle" in the context of light moving through glass?
column5: The angle where light turns into a rainbow
column6: The angle at which light no longer passes through and reflects back inside
column7: The angle of the sun at noon
column10: The critical angle is the specific angle of incidence that results in total internal reflection where light stays inside the medium.

column4: When light waves bend around the edges of a narrow opening what is this behavior called?
column10: Diffraction is the bending or spreading of waves as they encounter an obstacle or pass through a narrow slit.

column4: In the formation of a rainbow which color appears on the outermost part of the arc?
column10: Red light appears on the outside because it refracts (bends) the least when entering and leaving the raindrops.

column4: If you move the screen farther away from a double slit what happens to the interference pattern?
column7: The fringes spread out and become wider
column10: Increasing the slit-to-screen distance gives the light waves more space to spread out resulting in wider fringe separation.

column4: What type of interference results in dark bands on a screen?
column10: Dark bands occur where waves meet out of phase (crest meets trough) and cancel each other out which is destructive interference.

column4: Why is a rainbow shaped like a bow or arc in the sky?
column6: The raindrops are falling in a curve
column7: Light reaches your eyes at specific angles from a collection of drops
column10: The arc is a result of the specific angles at which light is refracted and reflected back to the observer from many raindrops.

column4: What happens to a light ray that hits a surface exactly along the normal line (0 degrees)?
column6: It bends away from the normal
column7: It passes through or reflects straight back without bending
column10: Light entering or hitting a surface at 0 degrees to the normal does not bend because all parts of the wavefront reach the surface at the same time.

column4: In the ROYGBIV sequence which color bends the most when passing through a prism?
column10: Violet light has the shortest wavelength and slows down the most in glass causing it to refract at the greatest angle.

column4: Why is it difficult to observe light diffraction through a doorway compared to sound diffraction?
column6: Light waves are much smaller than the width of a doorway
column10: Diffraction is most noticeable when the opening is similar in size to the wavelength. Light wavelengths are extremely small compared to common openings like doors.

column4: A student observes that the 2nd bright fringe in an interference pattern is farther from the center than the 1st. What is the spacing between them like?
column5: The spacing gets larger as you move out
column6: The spacing is roughly even between consecutive fringes
column7: The spacing gets smaller as you move out
column10: In a standard double-slit interference pattern the fringes are generally spaced at regular equal intervals.

column4: What does the "symmetrical" nature of an interference pattern mean?
column5: The left side is a mirror image of the right side
column6: The top is different from the bottom
column7: The pattern only has one bright spot
column8: The lines are all different colors
column10: Symmetry means the distances to the bright and dark fringes are the same on both the left and right sides of the central maximum.

column4: What happens to a light ray when it undergoes "Total Internal Reflection"?
column5: It passes through the surface and slows down
column6: It is completely absorbed by the material
column7: It reflects entirely back into the original material
column10: Total internal reflection occurs when the incident angle exceeds the critical angle and 100% of the light reflects back into the original material.

column4: Which of the following describes a key difference between electromagnetic waves and mechanical waves?
column5: EM waves travel slower than sound
column6: EM waves require a medium like water to travel
column7: EM waves can travel through a vacuum (empty space)
column8: EM waves do not have a frequency
column10: Unlike sound or water waves EM waves consist of oscillating electric and magnetic fields that do not need a physical medium to propagate.

column4: What is the correct order of the electromagnetic spectrum from longest wavelength to shortest wavelength?
column5: Radio Microwaves Infrared Visible UV X-rays Gamma
column6: Gamma X-rays UV Visible Infrared Microwaves Radio
column7: Visible UV Infrared Radio Microwaves Gamma X-rays
column8: Radio Visible Infrared UV Microwaves X-rays Gamma
column10: The spectrum is organized by wavelength and frequency; radio waves have the longest wavelengths while gamma rays have the shortest.

column4: Using the equation c = λ × f if the wavelength of an EM wave decreases while traveling through a vacuum what must happen to its frequency?
column10: Since the speed of light (c) is constant in a vacuum wavelength and frequency are inversely proportional; as one goes down the other must go up.

column4: Which type of radiation has enough energy to remove electrons from atoms and potentially damage biological tissue?
column10: High-frequency waves like UV X-rays and gamma rays are ionizing meaning they carry sufficient energy to disrupt atomic structures and DNA.

column4: Why are radio waves preferred for AM/FM broadcasting and television signals?
column6: Their long wavelengths allow them to bend around obstacles and the Earth’s curvature
column7: They travel faster than other EM waves
column8: They are easily absorbed by water molecules
column10: Longer wavelengths are better at diffracting around large objects making them ideal for wide-area broadcasting.

column4: What property of microwaves makes them suitable for satellite communication?
column5: They travel in straight focused beams (line-of-sight)
column6: They can bend around the entire planet
column7: They have the lowest frequency in the spectrum
column8: They are invisible to the human eye
column10: Microwaves have shorter wavelengths than radio waves allowing them to be directed in concentrated beams toward specific satellites.

column4: Why is visible light used in fiber-optic cables for high-speed internet?
column5: It has a lower frequency than radio waves
column6: It can travel through solid lead
column7: Its high frequency allows for rapid data transmission through glass fibers
column8: It is the only wave that can travel through a vacuum
column10: Higher frequency waves like visible light can carry significantly more information per second than lower frequency radio waves.

column4: Which type of EM wave is emitted as heat by objects and used in thermal imaging?
column10: Infrared radiation is associated with thermal energy; sensors can detect these wavelengths even in total darkness to see heat signatures.

column4: Why can X-rays be used to see bone fractures but not to transmit cellular phone signals?
column5: X-rays are too slow for communication
column6: X-rays are non-ionizing and safe for constant use
column7: X-rays are high-energy waves that penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by bone
column10: X-rays have very high energy and short wavelengths which allow them to penetrate soft tissue but their ionizing nature makes them unsafe for constant communication use.

column4: What is the speed of all electromagnetic waves in a vacuum?
column10: B
column11: All EM waves travel at the speed of light which is approximately 300
column13: 000 meters per second in a vacuum.

column4: Which of these is a common application for Ultraviolet (UV) radiation?
column10: UV light has enough energy to kill bacteria and is frequently used for sterilizing and purifying water.

column4: A radio wave has a wavelength of 2 meters. What is its frequency?
column10: Using f = c / λ (3 x 10^8 / 2) the frequency is 1.5 x 10^8 Hz or 150 MHz.

column4: Why is sunscreen used to protect the skin from UV rays but not from radio waves?
column6: UV rays are ionizing radiation that can damage DNA
column8: Radio waves are blocked by the atmosphere
column10: UV rays are high-frequency ionizing waves that can cause skin damage and cancer whereas radio waves are non-ionizing and safer.

column4: Which part of the EM spectrum is the only one detectable by the human eye?
column10: Visible light (400–700 nm) is the narrow range of the electromagnetic spectrum that human eyes can perceive.

column4: If a telecommunications engineer needs a wave that can carry a lot of data but is resistant to interference from other electronics which would they choose?
column10: Fiber optics using visible light pulses provide high bandwidth and are immune to the electromagnetic interference that affects radio and microwaves.

column4: What happens to the energy of an EM wave as its frequency increases?
column10: Energy is directly proportional to frequency; higher frequency waves like Gamma rays carry significantly more energy than Radio waves.

column4: Which EM wave is used in remote controls to change TV channels?
column10: Remote controls use low-energy infrared pulses to send signals to electronic devices.

column4: Why are gamma rays considered the most dangerous type of EM radiation?
column7: They have the highest frequency and can pass completely through the human body damaging DNA
column10: Gamma rays have the highest energy and shortest wavelength allowing them to penetrate deep into tissues and cause severe biological damage.

column4: Which wave property determines if a signal can bend around a hill in a remote village?
column10: Longer wavelengths (like those of radio waves) allow signals to diffract or bend around large physical obstacles like hills.

column4: In medical diagnostics why is MRI (Radio waves) often preferred over X-rays for frequent monitoring?
column6: Radio waves are non-ionizing and do not carry the radiation risks of X-rays
column7: Radio waves penetrate bone better than X-rays
column8: Radio waves are cheaper to produce
column10: Because radio waves are non-ionizing they do not damage cells or DNA making them safer for repeated diagnostic use compared to X-rays.
